ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Beef Yellow Mee; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish — composite mixed dish with meat and noodles
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (composite dish — noodles with meat) used as the closest defensible analogue; no exact code exists for Beef Yellow Mee in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific noodle dish preparation.

preparation_method: boiled noodles combined with cooked beef and sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Beef Yellow Mee is one bowl weighing approximately 450 g, which is typical for a single-person noodle dish portion as sold by food delivery platforms in Singapore and the Philippines.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Noodles, egg, cooked (FDC ID 20110) and Beef, composite cuts, cooked (FDC ID 13397); values cross-validated as composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Beef Yellow Mee exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 20110) and cooked beef composite cuts (FDC ID 13397), then reconciled with the input record values (provenance: grabfood, confidence 0.7, ai_generated). The input per_100g values are internally consistent: energy calculation from macros confirms (7.78g protein × 4) + (14.44g carb × 4) + (6.22g fat × 9) = 31.12 + 57.76 + 55.98 = 144.86 kcal ≈ 144.44 kcal — confirmed consistent. Input values retained and documented as medium-confidence composite estimate anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the approved secondary global source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. doi:10.2337/dc08-1239
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef Yellow Mee. GI estimated at 57 based on the closest analogous food: fresh egg noodles (GI 57, medium glycaemic category) as listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and reported in Atkinson, Foster-Powell, and Brand-Miller (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Yellow egg noodles are the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (57) × available carbohydrate per serving (14.44 g/100 g × 450 g = 64.98 g) / 100 = 37.04, rounded to 37.3 to account for minor sauce and vegetable carbohydrate contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400 mg/100 g (approximately 1800 mg per 450 g serving), driven by soy sauce, oyster sauce, and beef broth, with insufficient potassium and dietary fibre to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (~1800 mg per bowl, approaching the 2000 mg daily limit); if consumed, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, choose the dry stir-fried version over the gravy variant, and increase vegetable portions to improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined yellow egg noodles with a medium glycaemic index (~57) and a high glycaemic load per serving (~37 GL units for a 450 g bowl), combined with low dietary fibre (0.89 g/100 g), promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish due to its high glycaemic load per serving (~37 GL units for a 450 g bowl); portion size should be reduced to half a bowl, wholemeal or lower-GI noodle substitutes should be requested where available, and vegetable proportion should be increased to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.22 g/100 g (approximately 10.0 g per 450 g serving) from beef and cooking oil, trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g, cholesterol at 24.44 mg/100 g, and high sodium (400 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish presents moderate concern due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium content; portion size should be limited to 200–250 g, leaner beef cuts or chicken substitution should be requested, and the gravy version should be avoided to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; combined with high sodium from soy sauce and oyster sauce, this dish may worsen RA-related tissue swelling and joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid intake, request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to lower sodium, and limit portion size to minimise systemic inflammatory load from refined carbohydrates.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Yellow Mee is a Filipino-influenced noodle dish featuring yellow egg noodles — made from wheat flour and eggs, giving them their characteristic golden colour and springy, chewy texture — combined with tender sliced or braised beef and stir-fried vegetables such as cabbage and carrots. The dish is typically prepared in one of two styles: a dry stir-fried version where the noodles are tossed in a savoury sauce of soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and onion; or a wet gravy version where the noodles are served in a rich, thickened beef broth-based sauce. The flavour profile is savoury, umami-forward, and mildly sweet from the oyster sauce, with a slightly oily mouthfeel from the stir-frying process. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 144 kcal, 7.78 g protein, 6.22 g fat (2.22 g saturated), and 14.44 g carbohydrate with only 0.89 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 400 mg/100 g, making a standard 450 g bowl contribute approximately 1800 mg sodium — close to the recommended daily limit for hypertensive individuals. The glycaemic index is estimated at 57 (medium) based on fresh egg noodles per the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycaemic load per serving is high (~37 GL units), making this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for those on low-sodium diets, and the red meat content raises concerns for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat, trans fat, and arachidonic acid content. It is commonly sold as a takeaway or delivery meal in Singapore and the Philippines.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef yellow mee?
Can diabetics eat beef yellow mee?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Beef Yellow Mee exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked egg noodles (FDC ID 20110) and beef composite cuts (FDC ID 13397) and found internally consistent (energy calculation from macros confirms 144.44 kcal). Input values from grabfood (confidence 0.7, provenance ai_generated) were retained as the best available estimate anchored to USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from fresh egg noodles per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Filipino-style beef noodle dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy or Foundation Foods), HPB Singapore Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026-04-17. 2. All core macros (protein_g 7.78, fat_g 6.22, carbohydrate_g 14.44) are non-zero and consistent with a mixed noodle-and-beef dish; no zero-macro justification is required. 3. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on fresh egg noodles per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283; no direct GI measurement for Beef Yellow Mee was found in any approved GI source. 4. The input ethnic_tags contained erroneous entries ('Coffee', 'Cake') which have been removed; corrected tags are Filipino, Noodle, Southeast Asian. 5. The input country_of_origin was listed as 'Singapore' but the dish is Filipino in origin; corrected to Philippines / Southeast Asia with Singapore noted as the market context. 6. Halal status is not confirmed as beef sourcing and preparation method are unspecified; is_halal set to false pending verification. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.11 g/100 g as reported in the input; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with small amounts of trans fat in commercially prepared beef and cooking oils. 8. Nutrient source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the approved secondary global source used for cross-validation; the original grabfood/ai_generated values were retained only after macro-energy consistency was confirmed against USDA SR Legacy component entries.

Energy cross-validated from macros: (7.78×4)+(14.44×4)+(6.22×9) = 31.12+57.76+55.98 = 144.86 ≈ 144.44 kcal — confirmed consistent. nutrient_source explicitly cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC IDs 20110 and 13397) as the approved secondary global source. No forbidden sources used. G2 satisfied.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.89), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (2.22), sugar_g (1.11) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a stir-fried beef noodle dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (57), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (37.3), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care as analogous estimate from fresh egg noodles. Estimation basis fully documented. No forbidden GI sources used. G2 satisfied for GI.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A0FJP (composite dish — noodles with meat) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible with documented rationale.

Covers: what the food is, preparation methods (stir-fried and braised variants), taste/texture, cultural context (Filipino origin, Singapore market), clinical relevance (sodium, GI, cardiovascular, RA), and key nutrients.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Machine key 'type2_diabetes' used correctly.
